Sinapis arvensis, or wild mustard, is an annual plant in the Brassicaceae family, characterized by its tall stem, yellow flowers, and pungent smell. The plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various health issues, including digestive problems, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Sinapis arvensis include isothiocyanates, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which have been shown to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. The most common herbal preparations made with wild mustard include infusions, decoctions, and ointments, which are often used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne.
